author: jonny.derp
content: thanks for looking. note that the speedup will only be seen on GetPayload with that PR. All of the otel traces and benchmarks i have run with 72 blobs show a huge difference though.

author: jonny.derp
content: the difference is really only noticeable with a large number of blobs. i was testing it with 72 blobs, but you should still see a difference with less.

author: jonny.derp
content: okay nice! that lines up with what i was seeing. fwiw, i still think ssz would be a bit faster. i just wanted to point out that go's json encoding was pathologically slow

author: funnygiulio
content: Yeah that for sure. I am sure you can even do better

author: funnygiulio
content: Havent looked in the problem tho yet

author: jameshe5018
content: We have geth as a dependency in prysm already, wondering if it makes sense to export any of these functions so we would use the same for our end of blob json processing

author: jonny.derp
content: My guess is that unmarshaling doesn’t suffer from the same issue. The json.Marshal() problem comes from the validation/compaction pass that runs on already valid bytes, and in Geth’s RPC stack that ends up happening at two different layers. For unmarshaling, I’m thinking that it's a single parse pass, so there probably isn't redundant work to eliminate in the same way. I imagine that it's still a bit slower than ideal due to reflection, but not pathologically so.

author: jonny.derp
content: i could be wrong though. It'd be pretty easy to benchmark the marshalling and unmarshalling of the execution payload envelope with 72 blobs and see if unmarshalling is slow too.
